title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several routine items and made key decisions including awarding a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, adopting a resolution supporting state transportation funding, establishing $100/day fines for sanitary sewer discharge violations, and approving construction agreements for multiple road projects. The committee also received updates on CIP projects including a $800,000+ grant award for Birch Street reconstruction.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC annual report
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkkey Avenue
Construction service proposals for Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel projects

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Scofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. This 2029 project will likely be moved up to 2028. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Updates were provided on Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading, Scofield Avenue repairs by HOS Construction, and Fuller Street repairs by Melvin. A special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue was tentatively scheduled for May 28th at 6:00 PM.

Staff reported on spring activities including road limit removal on April 29th, mailbox and sign repairs, dewatering operations near Weston Avenue tributary for approximately one week, curb line sweeping, hot mix patching, storm damage cleanup from East Nick area, inlet repairs on Birch Street, and signing installation on Scofield Avenue. Culvert failures on Jones Street required installation of a third 18-inch culvert. Equipment maintenance included removing plow equipment and applying anti-rust treatment for summer operations.

Josh reported on water service repairs at Caribou Coffee, well maintenance, and meter card maintenance. Significant clear water infiltration was identified - April showed 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated, indicating approximately 15 million gallons of clear water entering the sanitary system. Staff identified sump pump discharges as a major contributor, particularly at the Kathleen lift station. The INI study with In-Pipe Technologies will help identify problem areas.

The committee acknowledged the annual report showing net operating income of approximately $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. The utility's value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. Cash reserves total $6.8 million. The utility serves 6,100 customers including 5,250 in Weston and 830 in Rothschild. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael presented a League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supporting long-term transportation funding solutions. He noted recurring annual costs for street maintenance and snow plowing aren't adequately funded by the state, requiring general tax dollar subsidies. Wisconsin remains affordable compared to neighboring states for automobile ownership costs. The committee unanimously recommended the village board adopt the resolution.

Michael explained the Ryan Street site stockpiles recycled concrete and asphalt that gets crushed into base course. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. Cost analysis showed the village saves approximately $11 per cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, resulting in about $130,000 savings across three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

The committee discussed enforcement of ordinance violations for illegal sump pump discharges to the sanitary sewer. After debate about willful versus unknowing violations, the committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines starting at $100/day. Trustee Zagami suggested waiting for the In-Pipe Technologies study to identify violators, then sending notices with 30-60 day correction periods. Motion passed unanimously to direct staff to create enforcement language not to exceed $100/day.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael presented an agreement allowing a duplex developer at 2230 Radkkey Avenue to temporarily connect via long lateral service until Radkkey Avenue reconstruction, when they would pay assessment for main extension. Committee amended the agreement to specify reconstruction no later than 2029 due to existing asbestos water main and poor road condition. Motion passed unanimously with the amendment.

The committee approved an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for construction on Military Road from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Weston's share for water and sewer relocation is estimated at $94,860. Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For the Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project, MSA bid $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910. For Bladel Avenue, MSA bid $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730. Staff recommended MSA for the roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel due to using full-time experienced staff rather than summer interns. Motion passed unanimously to approve both recommendations.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel for $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Village board to adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations at $100/day fine
Approve water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkkey Avenue with 2029 reconstruction deadline
Approve intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road construction ($94,860 Weston share)
Award construction services to MSA for Alderson/Jelnik project and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue project
Schedule Bladel Avenue special assessment meeting for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration impact for future meeting
Look at including street lighting for Scofield Avenue (Birch Street to Camp Phillips Road) in 2030 project
Norcon to begin concrete repairs on Scofield Avenue eastbound lanes starting Wednesday/Thursday

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially boosting attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Minutes of the previous meeting - April 21st
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated and elected Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated for Vice Chair, seconded by Jackson, and elected unanimously. Renee was nominated and elected Secretary unanimously.
Minutes from April 21st were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented the request. He reported 11,000-12,000 total attendance last year and announced that the competing Marshfield hot air balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making Taste and Glow the sole event in the area. Commission members questioned room usage, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. The commission requested better tracking of hotel room data for future applications. A motion to approve $5,000 (same as last year) was made, seconded by Jackson, and passed unanimously.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates and noted that Anastasia won the competition. This was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since approximately the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow organizers to provide hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved a site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and approved a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection districts. Both items passed unanimously.

agenda.item: Call to Order and Roll Call
Site Plan Review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court Facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Unfinished Business - Creation of Salt Storage Shed Inspection Program for Wellhead Protection District
April 2026 Staff Approved Certified Survey Map and Site Plan
Planning and Development Department Monthly Project Update Report

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court Facility Site Plan
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. Jennifer explained the building was rotated to address challenges with a gas pipeline easement running through the property and to reduce fill costs. Chief Jeremy noted the building will accommodate 50-60 officers for future growth beyond the current 40 officers. Discussion included the alternate storage shed placement for future building expansion, with commissioners suggesting moving it further north. A motion was made to recommend approval to the board of trustees subject to staff conditions 2-5. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt Storage Shed Inspection Program for Wellhead Protection District
discussions.body: Staff presented a program requiring two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and a winter operational inspection focusing on material handling practices. The program includes a standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards and a classification system for deficiencies. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and commissioners agreed staff should reach out to bring them into compliance. A motion to approve the program with staff reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee passed unanimously.
Motion to approve the April 13, 2026 meeting minutes as presented passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: April 2026 Staff Approved Survey Maps and Building Permits
discussions.body: Both the certified survey map report and building permit report were approved unanimously without discussion.

Jennifer announced the first joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. She will send calendar invites to commissioners.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, asking staff to waive excessive landscaping standards similar to what was done for the Greenheck Turner building. He also requested the commission require annual reports on salt shed inspections in wellhead protection zones, noting some zoning complaints have gone unaddressed for years.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive recommended for approval to Village Board of Trustees
Salt storage shed inspection program approved for implementation in wellhead protection districts
Staff to report inspection findings to Plan Commission and Public Works Committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg regarding wellhead protection compliance
First comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Jennifer to send calendar invites for steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be addressed in fall agenda

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new and returning members, featuring presentations from department heads covering the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Service Office, and Aging Disability Resource Center. The committee approved April 1st meeting minutes unanimously and received comprehensive overviews of department missions, budgets, and current initiatives including homelessness response efforts and veteran services.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote participation confirmation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operations functions required by statute, ordinance, resolution or policy
Review of standing committee ordinances - missions, duties, and responsibilities
Educational presentation - Social Services Department overview
Educational presentation - Veteran Service Office overview
Educational presentation - Aging Disability Resource Center overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion passed unanimously with all members voting in favor.

Administrator Lance Leonard provided an overview of the committee's structure under section 2.04 sub5 of county ordinances. He explained reporting relationships with three direct county departments (Veteran Service, Social Services, Health Department) and affiliated entities (North Central Health Care, Aging Disability Resource Center). Chair Boots noted the committee will be working on a new strategic plan to be completed by December.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented on the department's mission to prevent disease, promote health, and protect from environmental hazards. She highlighted over 1,200 licenses overseen, public health vending machines providing free resources including tick kits, the Nurse Family Partnership program now graduating its first two-year-olds, and a new FTE position for homeless system response. The department has approximately 37.6 FTE and about half of its $5 million budget comes from tax levy. Supervisor Kremes asked about pandemic preparedness, and Suginaka confirmed extensive plans are in place with staff trained from COVID experience.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who could not attend. He covered the department's divisions including child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. The department has nearly 140 FTE with a 2026 revenue budget over $22 million and about $7 million in tax levy. Leonard highlighted the successful Family Keys program addressing housing instability, which was piloted after state outreach and modified based on board feedback.

Veteran Service Officer Aaron Galindo reported his office increased to 3.0 FTE in 2025. Customer interactions jumped from approximately 5,900 in early 2025 to 8,149 by year end. He reported $44 million annually in benefits coming to local veterans and $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Galindo discussed housing stability initiatives with Center for Veterans Issues and suicide prevention efforts, noting Marathon County had 30 suicides in 2025 with 7 being veterans. Supervisor Kremes asked about inter-county coordination, and Galindo explained veterans can work with any county's VSO and his office has good relationships with neighboring counties.

Executive Director Mike Ray presented on the four-county ADRC serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties with approximately 60 FTE and 385-400 volunteers. He highlighted significant financial challenges with nutrition program costs up 40-45% over four years while federal funding remained flat. The Older Americans Act hasn't been reauthorized in over two years. Marathon County's contribution is $413,135. Ray noted the organization hadn't requested a levy increase from 2011 until 2024. Vice Chair Rosenberg praised the culturally appropriate meals program at the Hmong American Center.

actionItems: April 1st committee meeting minutes approved unanimously
North Central Healthcare will be scheduled for a separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report with 2025 data to full board
Strategic plan development to be discussed at upcoming meetings with goal of completion by December
Next meeting corrected to Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (not May 29th as initially stated)
Committee members invited to submit agenda items via email to Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinances including amendments to mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and held extensive discussion about farmers market parking issues at Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees called to order, Pledge of Allegiance, Roll Call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Department reports (Administrative, Clerk, Finance, Police, Parks, Planning, Public Works, Technology)
Consent agenda including vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, standing committees, nuisances, and e-bikes
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Park and Recreation Impact Fee Amendment
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed fee increases from $300 per single family unit to $761, from $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Trustee Barb supported the fees as justified given population increases. Jim Pitel during public comment criticized the lack of detailed data on actual park maintenance costs. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passed unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passed unanimously.

The board debated amendments to section 1.08 of the officials handbook. Discussion centered on removing specific health/safety/welfare justification requirements to make the policy more broad. Trustees agreed chairs should not have to evaluate excuses. Beck moved to amend by striking sentences requiring health/safety reasons, seconded by Clark. The amendment passed with two no votes from Hermling and Lope Sorrell. The original motion as amended also passed with two no votes from the same trustees.

Chief Finkey explained the ordinance creates a reciprocal inspection agreement between Safer, Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Clover, and Riverside. Under this system, a fire inspection sticker from any participating department is recognized by all others, eliminating redundant inspections for food trucks operating in multiple communities. Motion by Lope Sorrell, approved unanimously.

discussions.item: Secondhand Dealer License for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC
discussions.body: Discussion addressed why secondhand dealer licenses are required - primarily for tracking potentially stolen merchandise through a police-accessible database and requiring a 30-day hold period. Since the business at 5703 Jamar Street hasn't received occupancy yet, the board amended the motion to make approval contingent on satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit. Approved unanimously as amended.

discussions.item: Farmers Market Location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: Extensive discussion addressed parking concerns at the new Kennedy Park location after the previous Camp Phillips/Scoffield site was sold for development. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about cars parking on grass along Alderson. The market has grown from 25 stalls to 53 vendors who can now keep vehicles with them. Renee explained the market is not losing money and receives grants for SNAP/EBT token programs. No formal action taken; discussion only as this was an evolving situation to monitor.

Public Works Director Michael reported on efforts to resolve the speed limit issue raised at previous meetings. The DOT engineering study indicated 35 mph, but traffic data showed 85th percentile speed at 45 mph. Construction delayed completing a second speed study. A resident noted the current speed is inconsistent with the village ordinance. Staff indicated this would need to come back to the board, possibly at a joint meeting.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street offered public comment on multiple topics. He criticized spending $108,000 on outside engineering consultants (agenda items 68-69) when an engineering technician position remains unfilled, questioning why the administrator hasn't filled approved positions. He objected that Lisa Beck was introduced as a Safer board member at the April Fire Department meeting before being officially appointed. He criticized the lack of information on Kennedy Park in the meeting packet despite trustees requesting a detailed report. During the impact fee hearing, Pitel argued the village lacks detailed data on actual park maintenance costs to justify the fee amounts.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased to $761 single family, $563 duplex, $495 multifamily effective with ordinance passage
Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Golden Mua and Stephanie Daniels appointed to CLIPS committee
Comprehensive Plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 PM as joint meeting with Plan Commission
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement for building inspection assistance to Town of Ringle for tornado-damaged homes
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove health/safety justification requirements
Speed limit study for Weston Avenue to be completed after construction ends and brought back to board
Committee/commission applications to be included in board packets going forward
Electronics drive date corrected to June 6th
Secondhand dealer license for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC approved pending occupancy and background check

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held its adjourned organizational meeting, approving committee appointments and electing Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to at-large executive committee positions. The board also unanimously approved a resolution to reduce landfill tipping fees for debris related to the recent tornado and flooding disaster that devastated the Ringle area, with fees reduced from $65/ton to approximately $36.94/ton through June 17th.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Committee appointments discussion and remarks from Chair
Election of at-large executive committee members
Consent agenda items C10 through C14A
Resolution D15 - Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution D16 - Social services budget amendment
Resolution E17 - Landfill tipping fee modification for disaster relief
Vote on suspension of rules for tipping fee resolution

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs delivered remarks thanking staff and Vice Chair Boots for developing committee appointments, emphasizing that 34% of the board is in its first or second term. He outlined goals including increasing diversity of perspective, providing leadership development opportunities, and honoring supervisor preferences. The motion to approve all committee appointments was made by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, and carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Supervisor Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg spoke in support of Supervisor Leur, citing her advocacy for 'our county's smallest children and our oldest elders.' Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors voted for two candidates. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on the consent agenda. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26 Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal from the Solid Waste Management Board to reduce tipping fees for disaster-related debris following the April tornado and flooding. The standard $65/ton fee would be reduced to approximately $36.94/ton through June 17th, with the state waiving $13.10 and the Town of Ringle waiving $2.33. Two residents, Jessica Tolesie and Matt Haybeck, were granted special permission to speak, requesting the county waive fees entirely for affected homeowners, with Tolesie noting 'We didn't see anyone from Marathon County' during cleanup and asking for six months of fee waivers. Leonard noted the county would not meet the $670,743 damage threshold for state disaster aid reimbursement. The board first voted unanimously to suspend rules to hear the resolution without committee review, then approved Resolution 26-26 unanimously. Motion by Supervisor Arstead, seconded by Supervisor Morash.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution at Supervisor Rosenberg's request. Jessica Tolesie asked Marathon County to waive fees entirely for six months, noting that the state, DNR, and Town of Ringle had all waived their fees, and that private businesses had donated up to $40,000 in cleanup costs. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of affected homeowners, noting approximately 44 homes were damaged and many insurance adjusters had not yet arrived, requesting extended fee relief.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large executive committee positions
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster debris to approximately $36.94/ton through June 17, 2025
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with Community Foundation about potential reimbursement mechanism for unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid Waste Management Board directed to develop a clear policy within six months on handling similar disaster situations in the future
Board to revisit tipping fee reduction timeline at May meeting if extension beyond June 17th is needed

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only waive reconnect fees for septic systems for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing introduction and committee member introductions
Rezoning petition 5A1: Tim Veland on behalf of James Offer and David Tract - Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential in Town of Green Valley
Rezoning petition 5A2: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz - Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture in Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waivers for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Rezoning petition 5A1: James Offer and David Tract
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential in the Town of Green Valley. The Town Board recommended approval on March 10, 2026, and staff recommended approval as the rezoning meets all criteria and is consistent with the comprehensive plan. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and conclusions of law, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Rezoning petition 5A2: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture in the Town of McMillan. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature, and Pogle explained the property has a home and horse riding stable, and the rezoning would make the home a conforming use rather than non-conforming. He confirmed no natural area would be lost. The Town Board recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waivers for April 17, 2026 severe weather victims
discussions.body: Director Lori Miskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the tornado in the towns of Ringle, Easton, and villages of Kronenwetter and Weston. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concerns about fairness to others who experience disasters without emergency declarations. After significant discussion about costs and policy implications, Supervisor Drebeck proposed an amendment to limit the waiver to only reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems to existing working systems, excluding new system permits and zoning fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor Kurt and passed. The amended resolution was then approved to forward to the county board, though not unanimously.

Director David Hagenbusher presented an overview of the Solid Waste Department, explaining the landfill operations, tipping fee structure, renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, and upcoming challenges including a $6 million expansion project and required leachate treatment facility by 2032 due to PFAS regulations. Marilyn Bend praised the department for keeping costs competitive. The department serves Marathon, Portage, and Shawano counties and accepts approximately 1,000 tons daily.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department presentation
discussions.body: County Forest Administrator Tom Lovelin presented on behalf of Director Jamie, covering the department's 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees serving both Marathon County and City of Wausau. He highlighted Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million annually for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld announced Tom Lovelin is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service. Joe Tucker was selected as the new County Forest Administrator.

publicComment: No public comment was offered. No one signed up to speak during the public comment period.
actionItems: Rezoning approved for James Offer and David Tract property from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential in Town of Green Valley - forwarded to County Board
Rezoning approved for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz property from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture in Town of McMillan - forwarded to County Board
Modified fee waiver resolution limiting waiver to septic system reconnect fees only for April 17, 2026 tornado victims forwarded to County Board for consideration
CPZ staff to track costs and requests related to tornado fee waivers for future policy development
Conservation services educational presentation to be scheduled for summer meeting

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until next month, approved kayak launch signage for installation at village parks, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and the Yellow Banks disc golf course. Public commenters expressed opposition to dogs at the pool and criticism of Kennedy Park financial transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on Oaklair River Trail discussion

discussions.body: Minutes were approved with a motion and second. No discussion. Motion carried unanimously.

Sean Oster-Brine presented a timeline document showing the Kennedy Park master plan process from 2023 to present, including the May 9th ribbon cutting for Ray Jack and Western Fields. Scott asked clarifying questions about village capital funds, learning there are currently no earmarked capital dollars for Kennedy Park construction as those items were pulled pending the master plan project. Discussion noted the Friends of Kennedy Park are fundraising for lights on the upper field, currently around $85,000 toward a $117,000 goal, and plan to make a public check presentation at their June tournament. Committee members emphasized that master plans are guidance documents subject to change and that the volunteer Friends group deserves grace while fundraising.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and maps showing fallen trees removed from the river and installation of wooden mats over gas pipelines to protect them during equipment access. Dan explained the mat installation process and showed pictures of the work. The mats are now village property with some extras available for other projects.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to next month because Committee Chair Katrina was absent due to an unforeseen issue. The chair stated she should have the opportunity to speak on this issue she had requested. One public commenter (Andy from 6504 [address]) had expressed strong opposition during public comment, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.'

Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete due to limited equipment access - pressure washers cannot reach the trail location. Best removal tool is a sandblaster but the trailer is too wide for the bridges. Discussion included possible anti-vandalism coatings and seal coats to make future cleaning easier. Committee members suggested volunteer work days, noting many people love the trail. No formal action was taken; staff will explore options and report back.

Dan from Marathon County (2625 Northwestern Avenue) presented version 7 of signage for kayak launches along the river. Signs will be installed at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks within Village of Weston, plus locations in City of Schofield. The Highway J launch will likely be closed all season due to bridge replacement. The signs are funded by Marathon County Parks Foundation through state grants at no cost to the village. Motion to approve installation of the signs passed unanimously.

Sean reported that aquatic center pool repairs were completed, pool filling began and should be complete by end of day tomorrow, with opening day scheduled for June 6th. Dugout painting at Kennedy Park was completed. He noted his previous report incorrectly stated $10,000 from Marathon County Parks when it was actually $15,000, which was corrected in the minutes. The Mock Miller Park master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler next Tuesday, and a bike and pedestrian plan open house is scheduled for June 11th.

publicComment: Two public comments were offered. Andy (6504 [street]) spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period. I like dogs, but it's not a place for them. We have rivers. We have lakes. That's where they belong.' Jim Pensan (5002 [street], Weston) spoke online under the name 'Vern M', criticizing the 4 p.m. meeting time as demonstrating 'zero desire for interaction and input from the general public' and questioning Kennedy Park financial reporting, calling the $8,700 figure 'too coincidental' and asking 'are the friends of Kennedy Park maybe frauds of Kennedy Park because they won't actually tell us where the money went?'
actionItems: Minutes from April 27, 2026 approved
Dog Day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Staff to explore graffiti removal and prevention options for Oaklair River Trail
Yellow Banks kayak launch signs approved for installation - to be forwarded to village board
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting scheduled with Rettler
Food allergy awareness playground signage to be brought back for future discussion
June 22, 2026 set as next meeting date

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged financial reports for April. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation by Municipal Clerk Pam on the clerk's office operations, elections, licensing, and budget. Committee member Evan Erickson announced his resignation as he is moving out of Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview
Old business, new business, staff remarks

discussions.body: Motion to approve minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. Motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

Jessica provided an update on TIF closure timeline, anticipating October-November for final bond payoff. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, which staff hopes to complete this summer. A committee member confirmed the final numbers still look good and the village will be able to pay off the debt.
Motion to acknowledge items 5, 6, and 7 (April budget status report, TIF reports, and legal details report) was made by Daniels and seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented extensively on clerk office operations including elections, licensing, tax collection, and the quarterly newsletter. Key points included: the office has reduced from 4 full-time staff to 2 full-time and 1 part-time; they handled 84% of tax collections ($22 million) at the counter; pet licensing was brought back in-house from Pet Data saving costs; and the newsletter costs approximately $30,000 annually for postage and printing. Discussion occurred about potentially increasing chief election inspector pay from $13 to $15/hour to match county averages. Committee members asked about the pet license fee increase from $10 to $20, which Pam explained was due to Marathon County raising fees to cover their Humane Society costs.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market has been going well with full vendor spots. She has been present for 6-7 hours on Saturdays starting at 6 AM. Vendors expressed satisfaction with the new setup and location. Discussion addressed parking concerns on Alderson and Newport streets, with staff clarifying that only the west side of Alderson allows parking while Newport has never had parking restrictions.

Evan Erickson announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. He thanked committee members for the opportunity to serve. The chair noted there are now open spots on this and other committees and encouraged outreach to interested citizens.

actionItems: CDA resolution needed this summer to call TIF bonds ahead of October 1st payoff deadline
Pam indicated she will likely propose increasing chief election inspector pay to $15/hour for the next two-year election term
Committee seeking citizen volunteers to fill open committee spots
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met on May 4th, recommending approval of nuisance code amendments and an e-bike ordinance (with emoto prohibition added), advancing mobile food vendor fire inspection requirements, and authorizing a raze order for an abandoned structure. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations but deferred action for further staff analysis.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Acknowledgment of department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Mobile food vendor fire inspection requirements

discussions.body: Jim Senalt spoke regarding concerns about the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, predicting grass damage from vehicle parking. He questioned whether parking on grass violates village code and suggested ADA handicap spaces should be on asphalt rather than grass. He also questioned the need for new fire inspections on food trucks and expressed concern about long-standing code violations not being resolved.

Staff reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement list after the property sold at auction and the new owner submitted repair permits. Police Chief Hunt reported 2,394 calls for March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations issued. The department has hired up to its 40th officer position. Motion to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 was made by Sherwanka and passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments to nuisance ordinances, including switching tree limb height requirements to 14 feet over streets and 10 feet over walkways, and adding compliance language for jurisdictional authority. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously.

Committee discussed the model e-bike ordinance for Chapter 82. Chief Hunt strongly recommended prohibiting emotos (electric dirt bike-style vehicles) from roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property due to safety concerns, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Discussion touched on electric scooters but the committee decided to leave officer discretion for minor violations. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Sherwanka, to recommend approval with the addition of prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour overnight parking window (4am-4pm, October 31-May 1) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities, which typically have 4-5 hour windows. Public Works Director Michael Wodalski suggested eliminating the blanket regulation and instead signing 6-8 specific problem areas. Chief Hunt preferred maintaining overnight restrictions to give officers time to clear vehicles before morning plowing. Committee deferred action, directing staff to identify specific problem areas for targeted signage and bring recommendations back in July.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest High School parking lot. Staff estimated a basic crosswalk would cost approximately $2,000-3,000, while a lighted system would be $25,000-30,000. Committee agreed to support the crosswalk only if Mount Olive pays for installation. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Sherwanka, to recommend board approval contingent on Mount Olive covering costs passed unanimously.

Fire Chief presented a regional inspection program involving Marshfield, Stevens Point, Wisconsin Rapids, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Safer departments. Under the program, any participating department can inspect a food truck and issue a sticker valid across all municipalities. Chief noted one recent inspection failed due to improper LP gas piping and unsecured propane tanks. Motion by Sherwanka, seconded by Campkey, to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Staff presented three address corrections needed for E911 compliance: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence, and 2211 Radkey Avenue (new duplex on private drive). Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze proceedings on an abandoned structure that has been vacant since 2010-2014. A previous fire department training burn was canceled due to unsafe structural conditions. Staff noted chickens had been housed in the structure during winter. Current owners have agreed to the raze order with costs placed on their tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke during public comment expressing concerns about the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, predicting grass damage from parking vehicles on grass. He questioned whether this violates village parking-on-grass prohibitions and suggested ADA parking spaces should be on asphalt. He also questioned the origin of new fire inspection requirements for food trucks and expressed frustration about long-standing code violations appearing on reports without visible progress.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to Board of Trustees for approval
Recommend e-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition to Board of Trustees for approval
Staff to identify specific problem areas for overnight parking signage and return with recommendations in July
Public Works Director to contact Mount Olive Church regarding crosswalk cost-sharing; if they agree to pay, recommend board approval
Recommend Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections to Board of Trustees
Recommend address corrections to Board of Trustees for E911 compliance
Authorize staff to begin raze order proceedings for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property conditions at Sternberg and South Timber (furniture and dumpster complaints)
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.

Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031

title: Water Works Commission Meeting

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
